explicit_text: I am Dr. Wolfgang Liedtke, a neurologist and scientist physician at Duke University and Duke Health Systems. For over 15 years, I have specialized in treating chronic nerve pain, especially oral facial pain, and I run a research laboratory focused on its causes and mechanisms. The COVID-19 pandemic deeply affected my clinical practice and research, disrupting years of progress with my patients despite few of us actually contracting the virus. I adapted by practicing remotely and continuing to support my patients through these challenging times.
implicit_text: I am a neurologist and scientist physician at a major research university and health system in North Carolina. For over 15 years, I have specialized in treating chronic nerve pain, especially oral facial pain, and I run a research laboratory focused on its causes and mechanisms. The global viral outbreak deeply affected my clinical practice and research, disrupting years of progress with my patients despite few of us actually contracting the virus. I adapted by practicing remotely and continuing to support my patients through these challenging times.

explicit_text: I have been working at Duke University for about 15 years as a neurologist and scientist physician. Duke provides the clinical and research environment where I treat patients with chronic oral facial pain and run my research lab. During the COVID-19 pandemic, I shifted much of my clinical practice to remote consultations from my home office, which was supported by the infrastructure at Duke. This institution has been central to both my clinical work and scientific publications.
implicit_text: I have been working at a prestigious university in Durham, North Carolina for about 15 years as a neurologist and scientist physician. This institution provides the clinical and research environment where I treat patients with chronic oral facial pain and run my research lab. During the global viral outbreak, I shifted much of my clinical practice to remote consultations from my home office, which was supported by the infrastructure here. This place has been central to both my clinical work and scientific publications.

explicit_text: The COVID-19 pandemic had an extraordinary and extreme impact on my life and work, even though neither I nor most of my patients contracted the virus initially. It disrupted the progress I had made over many years in helping patients with chronic pain, as the psychological stress, anxiety, and isolation caused by the pandemic greatly magnified their pain. I had to adapt my practice to remote consultations and manage the emotional toll on both my patients and myself. The pandemic also delayed my research work and publication processes significantly.

entity: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
explicit_text: My laboratory had a paper accepted by the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS) during the pandemic. Normally, revisions would have taken a few weeks, but due to COVID-19 restrictions and lab closures, the process extended over a year. I communicated with the journal to explain the situation and negotiated to have the paper published with a qualifier about the pandemic. This experience highlighted how the pandemic severely impacted research productivity and funding timelines.
implicit_text: My laboratory had a paper accepted by a leading national scientific journal during the viral outbreak. Normally, revisions would have taken a few weeks, but due to restrictions and lab closures, the process extended over a year. I communicated with the journal to explain the situation and negotiated to have the paper published with a qualifier about the pandemic. This experience highlighted how the outbreak severely impacted research productivity and funding timelines.

explicit_text: Some of my patients live in Georgia, and before the pandemic, I arranged remote consultations for those who would have been severely stressed by traveling. This was because travel often aggravated their chronic pain, especially nerve pain of the head and face. The experience with patients in Georgia helped me adapt to remote medicine, which became essential during the COVID-19 pandemic when in-person visits were limited.
implicit_text: Some of my patients live in a southeastern U.S. state known for its peaches, and before the viral outbreak, I arranged remote consultations for those who would have been severely stressed by traveling. This was because travel often aggravated their chronic pain, especially nerve pain of the head and face. explicit_text: I have patients in Florida who suffer from chronic nerve pain, and similar to those in Georgia, I provided remote consultations to avoid the stress of travel. This approach was initially to accommodate their pain conditions but became a crucial part of my practice during the COVID-19 pandemic. Managing patients remotely from my home office in Florida and other locations allowed me to continue care safely and effectively.
